# Terras de Gaia - Development Instructions

## Project Overview

Terras de Gaia is a modern, high-performance news platform built with Next.js 16, React 19, and TypeScript, connected to a WordPress CPanel backend via REST API. The frontend follows a modern newspaper design with dark theme (#0a0c10 bg, #00a6f0 accent).

**Core Stack:**
- Next.js 16.2.4 (App Router with Server Components)
- React 19 + TypeScript
- Framer Motion (animations)
- Tailwind CSS 4
- shadcn/ui components
- Lucide React icons

## Architecture Principles

### DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)
- Extract reusable components for common patterns
- Use shared utilities in `/lib` for data fetching and transformation
- Implement post enricher pattern for data normalization
- Cache at multiple levels (server ISR, client-side)

### KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid)
- Prefer composition over complex inheritance
- Use Next.js built-in features (ISR, SSR, SSG) appropriately
- Minimize component prop drilling; use context only when necessary
- Keep components under 300 lines; split if larger

### Frontend Design Pattern
Modern newspaper aesthetic with:
- Dark theme with high contrast (white text, #0a0c10 background)
- Accent color: #00a6f0 (cyan blue)
- Typography: Uppercase italic headers, monospace sections
- Grid layouts for content organization
- Smooth animations and transitions (Framer Motion)

## WordPress Integration & Security

### Authentication
All WordPress API requests must use Basic Auth headers:

```typescript
function getAuthHeaders() {
  const credentials = `${WP_USER}:${WP_APP_PASSWORD}`;
  const base64Credentials = Buffer.from(credentials).toString('base64');
  return { 'Authorization': `Basic ${base64Credentials}` };
}
```

### Environment Variables (Required)
```
NEXT_PUBLIC_WORDPRESS_API_URL=https://[domain]/wp-json/wp/v2
WP_USER=[your_app_user]
WP_APP_PASSWORD=[your_app_password]
```

### Rate Limiting & Caching
- Server-side: ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) with `revalidate: 180` (3 minutes default)
- Client-side: Use React Query or custom cache for API calls (5-10 min TTL)
- Rate limits: 10 requests/minute per endpoint (enforce in API routes)

## Folder Structure & Conventions

```
app/                          # Next.js App Router pages
├── api/                       # API routes (authentication, rate limiting)
├── [category]/
│   └── [slug]/page.tsx        # Dynamic pages
components/
├── ui/                        # shadcn UI components
├── [Feature]*.tsx             # Feature components (News, Programs, etc.)
└── [Feature]-*.tsx            # Sub-components (news-feed, post-card)
lib/
├── wp.ts                      # WordPress API functions
├── post-enricher.ts           # Data transformation
├── cache.ts                   # Caching utilities
└── utils.ts                   # General utilities
```

## Component Patterns

### Pattern 1: News Components
Used for: Posts, articles, news feeds
- Structure: Grid layout (left content + right sidebar)
- Data Flow: Server fetch → enrichment → client render
- Examples: news.tsx, news-feed.tsx, post-card.tsx
- Features: Infinite scroll, lazy loading, category badges

### Pattern 2: Programs Components
Used for: Scheduled programs, TV guides, episodes
- Structure: Grid/list layout with filters
- Data Flow: Fetch from WordPress custom post type
- Examples: programs.tsx, program-portal.tsx
- Features: Schedule integration, video players, filtering

### Pattern 3: Interactive Players
Used for: Podcasts, videos, media players
- Structure: Hero section + controls + visualizers
- Examples: test.tsx (GaiaWaves podcast player)
- Features: Framer Motion animations, dynamic waveforms

## Code Quality Standards

### TypeScript
- Strict mode enabled in tsconfig.json
- Always type component props with interfaces
- Use any only as last resort (document why)

### Components

Good pattern:
```typescript
interface PostCardProps {
  title: string;
  excerpt: string;
  imageUrl?: string;
  category: string;
}

export function PostCard({ title, excerpt, imageUrl, category }: PostCardProps) {
  return (/* component */);
}
```

### Styling
- Use Tailwind CSS classes (no inline styles unless Framer Motion)
- Dark theme color palette:
  - Background: #0a0c10
  - Accent: #00a6f0
  - Text: white, white/50, white/20 (opacity variants)
- Use uppercase italic for headings, font-black for emphasis

### API Functions

Good pattern with error handling:
```typescript
export async function getPostBySlug(slug: string): Promise<Post | null> {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(`${API_URL}/posts?slug=${slug}`, {
      headers: getAuthHeaders(),
      next: { revalidate: 180 }
    });
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error('API error');
    const posts = await res.json();
    return enrichPosts(posts)[0] ?? null;
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Failed to fetch post:', error);
    return null;
  }
}
```

## Performance Guidelines

### Image Optimization
- Always use Next.js Image component
- Set priority for above-the-fold images
- Define explicit dimensions to prevent CLS

### Caching Strategy
- Server: ISR (3-5 min for dynamic content) with `next: { revalidate: 180 }`
- Client: React Query with staleTime 5 minutes

### Code Splitting
- Use dynamic imports for heavy components
- Lazy load below-the-fold sections
- Monitor bundle size with npm run build

## Development Workflow

1. Create feature branch: git checkout -b feature/feature-name
2. Follow component structure using established patterns
3. Test responsiveness on mobile, tablet, desktop views
4. Security check: No hardcoded secrets, always use auth headers
5. Performance review: Check bundle size and load times
6. Commit message format: [feature/fix/refactor]: descriptive message

## When to Ask for Clarification

Ask before implementing if:
- Business logic contradicts established patterns
- Significant performance trade-offs are required
- Security implications are unclear
- New external dependencies are needed
